questions.question: What mean gradient threshold defines severe aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: The mean gradient greater than 40 mmHg on echocardiography is one of the hemodynamic criteria to classify aortic stenosis as severe, along with a valve area less than 1.0 cm².

questions.question: What echocardiographic parameter is used along with the mean gradient >40 mmHg?
questions.explanation: In the evaluation of severe aortic stenosis, the mean gradient >40 mmHg is complemented by the aortic valve area (<1.0 cm²) to confirm the severity, according to international guidelines.

questions.question: What is the main cause of elevated mean gradient in aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: Mean gradient >40 mmHg reflects significant left ventricular outflow tract obstruction, typically due to calcification and fusion of the aortic leaflets in degenerative aortic stenosis.

questions.question: What classic symptom is associated with aortic stenosis with a gradient >40 mmHg?
questions.explanation: Exertional dyspnea is one of the cardinal symptoms of severe aortic stenosis (gradient >40 mmHg), along with angina and syncope, due to pressure overload and diastolic dysfunction.

questions.question: Which carotid pulse finding is typical in severe aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: The parvus et tardus (weak and late) carotid pulse is characteristic of severe aortic stenosis with a gradient >40 mmHg, reflecting fixed obstruction to ventricular outflow.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice in severe symptomatic aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: Aortic valve replacement (surgical or transcatheter) is the definitive treatment for symptomatic severe aortic stenosis with gradient >40 mmHg, as it improves survival and symptoms.
